name: Inverse Trigonometric Functions
subject: Maths

sections.heading: 2.1 Need for Inverses & Principal Branches
sections.body:
The trig functions $\sin,\cos,\tan,\cot,\sec,\csc$ are not one-one on their natural domains, so inverses are defined by restricting the domain to a principal branch on which the function becomes bijective.

Standard principal-value branches (the range of the inverse function):

  • $\sin^{-1}x:[-1,1]\to\left[-\dfrac{\pi}{2},\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right]$
  • $\cos^{-1}x:[-1,1]\to[0,\pi]$
  • $\tan^{-1}x:\mathbb R\to\left(-\dfrac{\pi}{2},\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)$
  • $\cot^{-1}x:\mathbb R\to(0,\pi)$
  • $\sec^{-1}x:(-\infty,-1]\cup[1,\infty)\to[0,\pi]\setminus\{\pi/2\}$
  • $\csc^{-1}x:(-\infty,-1]\cup[1,\infty)\to\left[-\dfrac{\pi}{2},\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right]\setminus\{0\}$

sections.heading: 2.2 Self-Inverse Identities
sections.body:
For $x$ in the appropriate domain:

  • $\sin(\sin^{-1}x)=x,\;x\in[-1,1]$ and $\sin^{-1}(\sin x)=x,\;x\in[-\pi/2,\pi/2]$.
  • $\cos(\cos^{-1}x)=x,\;x\in[-1,1]$ and $\cos^{-1}(\cos x)=x,\;x\in[0,\pi]$.
  • $\tan(\tan^{-1}x)=x,\;x\in\mathbb R$ and $\tan^{-1}(\tan x)=x,\;x\in(-\pi/2,\pi/2)$.

Outside the principal branch, $\sin^{-1}(\sin x)$ may differ from $x$ by reflection / shift.

sections.heading: 2.5 Sum & Difference Formulae
sections.body:
For $\tan^{-1}$:

$\tan^{-1}x+\tan^{-1}y=\tan^{-1}\dfrac{x+y}{1-xy}$ when $xy<1$. If $xy>1$ and $x,y>0$, add $\pi$; if $xy>1$ and $x,y<0$, subtract $\pi$.

$\tan^{-1}x-\tan^{-1}y=\tan^{-1}\dfrac{x-y}{1+xy}$, $xy>-1$.

For $\sin^{-1}$ and $\cos^{-1}$:

$\sin^{-1}x+\sin^{-1}y=\sin^{-1}\!\left(x\sqrt{1-y^{2}}+y\sqrt{1-x^{2}}\right)$, valid when $x^{2}+y^{2}\leq1$ or $xy<0$.

$\cos^{-1}x+\cos^{-1}y=\cos^{-1}\!\left(xy-\sqrt{1-x^{2}}\sqrt{1-y^{2}}\right)$, valid when $x+y\geq 0$.

sections.heading: 2.6 Double-Angle Style Identities
sections.body:
For $\tan^{-1}$: $2\tan^{-1}x=\sin^{-1}\dfrac{2x}{1+x^{2}}=\cos^{-1}\dfrac{1-x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}=\tan^{-1}\dfrac{2x}{1-x^{2}}$ (each with its own domain restriction).

Useful conversions: $\sin^{-1}x=\cos^{-1}\sqrt{1-x^{2}}=\tan^{-1}\dfrac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}$ for $x\in[0,1]$.

sections.heading: 2.7 Graphs (Qualitative)
sections.body:
Each inverse trig graph is the reflection of the corresponding restricted trig graph in the line $y=x$.

  • $y=\sin^{-1}x$: increasing on $[-1,1]$, runs from $-\pi/2$ to $\pi/2$.
  • $y=\cos^{-1}x$: decreasing on $[-1,1]$, runs from $\pi$ to $0$.
  • $y=\tan^{-1}x$: increasing on $\mathbb R$ with horizontal asymptotes $y=\pm\pi/2$.

summary: Inverse trig functions are defined on principal branches that make trig functions bijective. Master the standard ranges, the self-inverse identity rule (only inside the principal range), the complementary pair $\sin^{-1}+\cos^{-1}=\pi/2$, sign/reflection rules, and the sum-of-arctangents formula $\tan^{-1}x+\tan^{-1}y=\tan^{-1}\tfrac{x+y}{1-xy}$ along with its $\pm\pi$ correction when $xy>1$.
pitfalls: Applying $\tan^{-1}x+\tan^{-1}y=\tan^{-1}\tfrac{x+y}{1-xy}$ blindly when $xy>1$ — must add or subtract $\pi$.
Writing $\sec^{-1}x$ over the whole real line — domain is $|x|\geq 1$.
Treating $\sin^{-1}x$ as $1/\sin x$ — that's $\csc x$, not the inverse.
